9HWY-ZVX: Born in Bedfordshire, England, Thomas Carter crossed to Virginia as a young immigrant and built a substantial life along the Rappahannock River. Through tobacco and credit he acquired the 800-acre Barford Plantation and hundreds more acres, becoming a prosperous tobacco planter. He served as a captain in the Lancaster militia, sat as a court justice, worked as an attorney and deputy clerk, and joined the Council of Burgesses before his death in 1700.
9VWN-LJZ: A lifelong Louisianan, Isaiah Fogleman was born in Avoyelles Parish and answered the call to military service in 1861 during the Civil War. He spent his years in central Louisiana, moving from rural Avoyelles to Alexandria in Rapides Parish, where he lived out his final days, dying there in 1916.
G8PD-HW6: Daniel Hankins was a Virginia man, born in Lunenburg County and living his life in the colony's southern reaches. He is remembered today as a recognized patriot of the American Revolution, honored by the Daughters of the American Revolution as Patriot A051089, and died in Pittsylvania County in 1796.
K2SJ-BCM: Edward FitzRandolph left Nottinghamshire as a boy, sailing to New England with his parents around 1630. He settled first at Scituate in Plymouth Colony, where he married Elizabeth Blossom and joined the church, then moved on to Barnstable on Cape Cod. A man of Quaker faith, he uprooted his large family once more around 1668 to help settle Piscataway in New Jersey, where he died in 1675.
KLFD-X85: Nicholas Ware Garrett was born in Fairfax County, Virginia, in 1765, and his family carried him south to South Carolina while he was still an infant. He made his life in Laurens County as a planter, acquiring some 250 acres along Warrior Creek on the Enoree River in 1792. Through more than half a century of census records he raised a remarkably large family before his death in 1846.
KXBR-FD6: Daniel Le Blanc left the Poitou region of France around 1645 for the New World, settling along the Rivière-au-Dauphin near Port-Royal in Acadia. There he married the widow Françoise Gaudet and raised six sons and a daughter, becoming a respected and prosperous farmer. Around 1690 he was named one of six commissioners overseeing the colony, and amid the wars between France and England he signed an oath of allegiance to the English crown shortly before his death.
L262-RS1: Truman Francis Shelton spent his life in and around Slaton and Lubbock on the Texas plains, where he was born in 1921. He registered for the draft in early 1942 and entered military service that September, joining the war effort of his generation, before returning home to Lubbock County, where he died in 2003.
L41Y-CJH: Born in Edgefield, South Carolina, in 1792, John Egbert Bell Bean Sr. lived a life of faith and migration. He answered a religious calling and served as a Baptist clergyman in Bienville Parish, Louisiana, by mid-century. He later moved on to Jasper, Texas, where he farmed his remaining years, dying there in 1871.
L5L7-Y6H: John Lee Fogleman was born in Cottonport, Louisiana, in 1895 and spent his whole life in the central part of the state. He registered for the draft during the First World War and again in 1942, twice ready to serve his country. He raised his family in the Alexandria and Pineville area of Rapides Parish, where he lived until 1979.
L6LN-RDY: Caleb Carr arrived in New England as a boy in 1635 aboard the Elizabeth Ann, settling in Newport in the new colony of Rhode Island. He devoted four decades to public service for Rhode Island and Providence Plantations, sitting as a justice of the Court of Quarter Sessions. In May 1695 he reached the colony's highest office as Governor, though he served only months before his death that December.
LB7N-H54: Born in Haverhill, Massachusetts, in 1656, Benjamin Singletery answered his town's call as a young man, serving as a soldier in King Philip's War and earning pay for protecting Haverhill and its people. He married Mary Stockbridge in 1678 and raised a large family. Late in life he undertook a long journey south, resettling near Summerville in the South Carolina lowcountry, where he died in 1697.
LBQV-T8P: Thomas Owsley II was born around 1697 in Stafford County, Virginia, the son of an English-born colonial officer and officeholder. He was still young when his father died, but he carried the family forward, marrying Anne West and raising ten children. He settled in what became Fairfax County and lived there until his death in 1751, leaving a will that provided for his family.
LCRW-4XM: Thomas Whicker Sr. was born in England and came to Virginia in 1685 as an indentured servant, trading four years of labor for his passage across the Atlantic. In 1689 he went to court to win release from that indenture, and afterward made a life of his own in Westmoreland County. He died there in 1704, leaving a will to provide for those who followed.
LDMX-FQC: John Jeter Sr. was born in England in 1686 to a family said to descend from French Huguenots who had fled religious persecution generations earlier. He immigrated to colonial Virginia at the start of the eighteenth century, settling some eight miles from Port Royal in Caroline County. Family tradition holds that he married twice and fathered an extraordinary twenty-two children, leaving a vast line of descendants.
LHKP-QQW: Born in Essex County, Virginia, in 1706, John Parks Sr. lived a long life that spanned the colonial era and the birth of a nation. He served Virginia during the Revolutionary War and farmed land along the Piney River before joining the migration south to Wilkes County, North Carolina. He died there in 1793, leaving a detailed will that divided his land and worldly goods among his children.
LJTM-BWM: Samuel Ingram was an early pioneer of Virginia's New River frontier, first appearing there in 1767 when he claimed a 300-acre tract near present-day Newbern. He expanded his holdings with land on Peak Creek and put down deep roots in the region. In 1777 he took up arms in military service during the Revolutionary War, and he died in Montgomery County, Virginia, in 1799.
LKSG-3GW: Thomas Carter Jr. was baptized in Hackney, near London, in 1650, the son of a Virginia colonist who died while Thomas was still a child. He carried the family line to the Isle of Wight in Virginia, where land had been conveyed to him as his late father's son. He married Magdalen Moore and lived there until his death in 1710.
LRZ9-MQY: Lafayette Adam Free Sr. was born on Hilton Head Island, South Carolina, in 1737 and lived a remarkably long life in the South Carolina lowcountry and upcountry. Settled in Fairfield County, he took up arms for his country during the War of 1812 and lived nearly a full century, dying in 1836.
LTZW-CBV: Elizabeth Foster was born in Spotsylvania County, Virginia, in 1694 and spent her entire life in that colonial community. She married twice, first to a Leavell and then to William Pemberton, and raised a family there. Her 1775 will, written under the name Elizabeth Vaughan, carefully remembered her four surviving children and the children of her late son Martin.
LW19-BJ5: John Haywood II was born in Barbados in 1684 to a family that had emigrated from Lancashire, England. He made his way through New York to North Carolina, settling around 1741 in the colony's northern counties. An engineer, legislator, and soldier, he served in the Colonial Assembly, championed the colony's first printing press, oversaw coastal fort construction, served as provincial treasurer, and commanded the Edgecombe County militia of over 1,300 men.
LY3D-QPM: Rachel Embree Carr was born in New Castle, Delaware, in 1665, in the early days of English settlement along the Delaware River. By 1692 she had made her home in Pennsylvania, living in Chester Township, where she spent the remainder of her life until her death in 1736.
LYR4-C9S: Jane M. Henderson began life in Manchester, England, in 1843, and crossed the Atlantic as a young child when her family immigrated in 1849. She made her home in New Orleans, Louisiana, where she lived for decades. She reached the venerable age of ninety-five, dying in Louisiana in 1938.
LZ8Y-9W2: Thomas Stackhouse was born in Giggleswick, Yorkshire, England, in 1660, and joined the early wave of Quaker settlers bound for William Penn's colony, arriving in Pennsylvania in 1682. He put down roots in Bucks County, where he served as a representative in the Colonial Assembly. He died at Langhorne in 1744 and was laid to rest in the Middletown Friends Cemetery.
LZJJ-WM6: Martha Sarah Garrett was born in Walton County, Georgia, in 1830 and joined the great westward migration to Texas, where she appears in Jasper County by 1860. Widowed in her later years, she moved on to the far reaches of West Texas, living in Pecos County. She died near Sheffield in 1906.
M4B5-NXF: Jarrett Menefee was born in Virginia in 1721 and lived through the founding of the nation. He served his country during the Revolutionary War in Colonel Benjamin Logan's company of the Virginia Regiment. Like many of his generation, he later joined the migration west across the mountains into Kentucky, where he died in Lincoln County in 1811 at the age of ninety.
MP9Q-CCB: Tabitha Stone lived through the dangers of frontier Massachusetts, born in Sudbury in 1655. She married Abraham Wheeler and raised children at Lancaster, an exposed settlement repeatedly struck by conflict. In September 1697 she was taken captive by Indians, an ordeal that left its mark on her family's history. She survived to return to Sudbury, where she died in 1719.
PZTD-JWC: John Evans Morgan was born in Nantmel, Radnorshire, Wales, in 1642, and earned his living as a broadweaver, a skilled cloth worker of his day. He left the British Isles for the New World and settled in Radnor Township in Chester County, Pennsylvania, among other Welsh emigrants, living there until his death in 1703.
